title: NLP Deep Dive
description: Natural Language Processing from word embeddings to LLM fine-tuning — with FAANG interview depth covering Word2Vec, GloVe, FastText, CRF, BiLSTM-CRF, Seq2Seq with attention, BERT variants, and parameter-efficient fine-tuning (LoRA, QLoRA).
details: NLP techniques span from distributed word representations (Word2Vec, GloVe) through sequence labeling (CRF, BiLSTM-CRF) to modern Transformer-based models (BERT, GPT). This guide covers the core algorithms, their mathematical foundations, training dynamics, and exactly how FAANG interviews test each concept. Each section includes intuition, concrete benchmarks, and practical workflows.

sections.title: Word Embeddings — Distributed Representations
sections.description:
One-hot → dense embeddings. One-hot vectors are orthogonal (|V| dimensions, zero similarity between any pair). Dense embeddings learn a distributed representation where each dimension captures a latent semantic feature. The result: king − man + woman ≈ queen because the vector space encodes analogies as arithmetic.

Word2Vec (Mikolov 2013): Two architectures — CBOW predicts the center word from context words (bag of surrounding n-grams), Skip-gram predicts context words from the center word. Skip-gram works better for rare words since it treats each context as a separate training example. The negative sampling objective maximizes: log σ(u_o · v_c) + Σk=1..K E[log σ(−u_k · v_c)] where u_o is the output vector of the observed word, v_c is the center word's input vector, and u_k are sampled negatives. Hierarchical softmax offers an alternative using a binary Huffman tree (O(log|V|) vs O(|V|)).

GloVe (Pennington 2014): Factorizes the word co-occurrence matrix using weighted least squares: J = Σ f(Xij)(wi · w̃j + bi + b̃j − log Xij)². The weighting function f clips very frequent co-occurrences (which carry less signal). GloVe combines global matrix factorization (like LSA) with local context windows (like Word2Vec).

FastText (Bojanowski 2016): Extends Word2Vec by representing each word as a bag of character n-grams (3–6 chars). cheaper = <che> <chea> ... <per> + whole word. This handles OOV words — if a word wasn't seen during training, its embedding can be composed from its subword n-grams. Captures morphology: cheapest shares cheap- with cheaper so their embeddings are naturally close.

Evaluation: Intrinsic — word similarity benchmarks (WordSim-353, SimLex-999, MEN) measure cosine similarity correlation with human judgments. Extrinsic — use embeddings as features in downstream tasks (NER, sentiment) and measure improvement. There is often a gap: better intrinsic scores don't always transfer to better task performance.

[Intuition]: "One-hot vectors are orthogonal — they know nothing about word relationships. Embeddings learn that king − man + woman ≈ queen because the vector space captures semantic dimensions like gender and royalty as orthogonal axes."

[Concrete Numbers]: "Word2Vec trained on Google News (100B tokens) produces 300-dim vectors for 3M words/phrases. Training takes ~1 day on a single machine with negative sampling (k=15). The resulting embeddings capture analogies: Paris − France + Italy ≈ Rome at ~75% accuracy on the SemEval analogy task."
sections.codeBlock:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

# Skip-gram with Negative Sampling (minimal implementation)
# Given center word c, predict context word o from vocabulary V.
# Objective: maximize log sigmoid(u_o · v_c) + sum log sigmoid(-u_k · v_c)

class SkipGramNegSampling(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, vocab_size, emb_dim=300):
        super().__init__()
        # Center word embeddings (input vectors v_c)
        self.center_emb = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, emb_dim)
        # Context word embeddings (output vectors u_o) — separate matrix
        self.context_emb = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, emb_dim)
        # Init weights from uniform [-0.5/dim, 0.5/dim]
        nn.init.xavier_uniform_(self.center_emb.weight)
        nn.init.xavier_uniform_(self.context_emb.weight)

    def forward(self, center, context, negatives):
        """
        center:    (batch,) center word indices
        context:   (batch,) positive context word indices
        negatives: (batch, K) negative sample indices
        """
        v_c = self.center_emb(center)                          # (B, D)
        u_o = self.context_emb(context)                        # (B, D)
        u_k = self.context_emb(negatives)                      # (B, K, D)

        # Positive score: log sigmoid(u_o · v_c)
        pos_score = torch.einsum('bd,bd->b', v_c, u_o)         # (B,)
        pos_loss = -F.logsigmoid(pos_score).mean()

        # Negative score: log sigmoid(-u_k · v_c)
        neg_score = torch.einsum('bd,bkd->bk', v_c, u_k)       # (B, K)
        neg_loss = -F.logsigmoid(-neg_score).mean()

        return pos_loss + neg_loss

# --- Usage ---
# model = SkipGramNegSampling(vocab_size=50000, emb_dim=300)
# optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=0.001)
# for center, context, negs in dataloader:
#     loss = model(center, context, negs)
#     optimizer.zero_grad(); loss.backward(); optimizer.step()
#
# Final embeddings: model.center_emb.weight (or average center + context)
# The two embedding matrices learn different representations;
# averaging them often yields slightly better downstream performance.

sections.title: Sequence Labeling — NER, POS, Chunking
sections.description:
Task: Given input sequence x₁..xn, predict label sequence y₁..yn. For NER, the standard annotation scheme is BIO tagging: B-PER (beginning of person name), I-PER (inside person name), B-ORG, I-ORG, O (outside any entity). The label set is O(|entity_types| × 2 + 1).

Hidden Markov Model (HMM): Generative model: P(X,Y) = Π P(yi|yi−1) · P(xi|yi). Viterbi decoding finds the most likely sequence in O(n·|Y|²). Strong independence assumption: each xi depends only on yi (no feature overlap).

Conditional Random Fields (CRF): Discriminative model: P(Y|X) = exp(Σ w·f(yi, yi−1, xi, i)) / Z(X). The partition function Z(X) = ΣY' exp(score(Y',X)) normalizes over all possible label sequences. CRF loss: −log P(Y|X) = −(Σ score) + log Z(X). No independence assumption on X — arbitrary overlapping features are safe. CRF is a log-linear model over transitions + emissions with global normalization.

BiLSTM-CRF (Huang 2015, Lample 2016): BiLSTM encodes context-sensitive word representations → CRF layer models label transition constraints. The BiLSTM replaces hand-crafted features with learned context vectors. CRF ensures globally coherent predictions: I-PER never follows B-ORG. This was SOTA for NER until Transformers.

Transformer-CRF: BERT embeddings → CRF layer. BERT's bidirectional context + CRF's global constraints combine to push state-of-the-art. For many tasks, a linear classification layer on top of BERT [CLS] is sufficient — CRF adds marginal gains on highly structured outputs.

[Concrete Numbers]: "CoNLL-2003 English NER benchmark: BiLSTM-CRF achieves 91.2% F1. BERT-CRF pushes to 93.6%. Human performance on this dataset is ~97%. The gap comes from rare entities, long-tail names, and ambiguous boundaries."

[Intuition]: "CRF matters for NER because labels have strong dependencies — I-PER always follows B-PER or I-PER, never B-ORG. A CRF captures these transition constraints globally, not per-token. Without CRF, a softmax over each token might predict I-ORG right after B-PER, which is impossible in the BIO scheme."
sections.codeBlock:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torchcrf import CRF  # pip install pytorch-crf

class BiLSTM_CRF(nn.Module):
    """BiLSTM + CRF for sequence labeling (NER, POS, chunking)."""
    def __init__(self, vocab_size, tagset_size, emb_dim=300, hidden_dim=256):
        super().__init__()
        self.embeddings = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, emb_dim)
        self.bilstm = nn.LSTM(
            emb_dim, hidden_dim // 2, num_layers=2,
            bidirectional=True, batch_first=True, dropout=0.3
        )
        self.proj = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, tagset_size)  # emissions
        self.crf = CRF(tagset_size, batch_first=True)

        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(0.3)

    def forward(self, x, tags=None, mask=None):
        """
        x:    (B, seq_len) token indices
        tags: (B, seq_len) gold labels (None at inference)
        mask: (B, seq_len) padding mask (1=valid, 0=pad)
        """
        emb = self.dropout(self.embeddings(x))          # (B, S, D)
        lstm_out, _ = self.bilstm(emb)                   # (B, S, H)
        emissions = self.proj(self.dropout(lstm_out))    # (B, S, T)

        if tags is not None:
            # CRF negative log-likelihood
            loss = -self.crf(emissions, tags, mask=mask)
            return loss
        else:
            # Viterbi decoding at inference
            return self.crf.decode(emissions, mask=mask)

# --- Usage ---
# model = BiLSTM_CRF(vocab_size=30000, tagset_size=9)  # 9 BIO tags for PER/ORG/LOC/O
# optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
# for batch in dataloader:
#     loss = model(batch['tokens'], batch['tags'], batch['mask'])
#     optimizer.zero_grad(); loss.backward()
#     torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), 5.0)
#     optimizer.step()
#
# At inference:
# predictions = model(batch['tokens'], mask=batch['mask'])
# predictions is a list of lists of predicted tag indices per sequence

sections.title: Text Classification — CNNs to Transformers
sections.description:
CNN for text (Kim 2014): 1D convolutions slide over word embedding sequences. Multiple filter widths (3,4,5) act as n-gram detectors — each filter learns to recognize a specific pattern (e.g., "not good" = negative sentiment). Max-over-time pooling extracts the most salient feature per filter, followed by a fully-connected classifier. Strengths: very fast, parallelizable, good for short texts. Weakness: limited context window size.

LSTM for text: Bidirectional LSTM reads text forward and backward → concatenate final hidden states → classification head. Attention pooling (weighted average of all hidden states) improves performance by letting the model focus on relevant tokens. LSTMs handle variable-length sequences naturally but are slower than CNNs.

Transformer for text: BERT takes the [CLS] token's final hidden state as the aggregate representation for classification. Fine-tuning: replace the classification head and update all parameters on the target task. DistilBERT achieves 97% of BERT's performance at 40% fewer parameters — a practical choice for latency-sensitive applications.

Hierarchical Attention (Yang et al. 2016): For document classification, word-level BiGRU + attention produces sentence vectors → sentence-level BiGRU + attention produces document vector. The attention weights are interpretable — you can visualize which words and sentences the model finds important.

[Intuition]: "CNNs for text act as n-gram detectors — each filter learns to recognize a specific pattern (e.g., 'not good'), regardless of position. The max-pooling ensures that if the pattern appears anywhere, the classifier sees it."

[Concrete Numbers]: "BERT-base fine-tuned on SST-2 (sentiment) achieves 94.5–95% accuracy in ~10 minutes on a single V100 GPU. DistilBERT reaches 92.5% at 2x the throughput. A CNN with word2vec embeddings and 3 filter widths (3,4,5) gets ~87% on the same task — the Transformer advantage is ~7–8 absolute points."
sections.codeBlock:
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForSequenceClassification
import torch

# BERT fine-tuning for text classification (SST-2 sentiment)
model_name = "bert-base-uncased"
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
model = AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained(
    model_name, num_labels=2  # binary: positive/negative
)

texts = ["This movie was fantastic!", "Terribly boring and slow."]

# Tokenize: padding + truncation to max_length=128
tokens = tokenizer(
    texts, padding=True, truncation=True, max_length=128,
    return_tensors="pt"
)

# Forward pass
with torch.no_grad():
    outputs = model(**tokens)  # logits: (B, 2)
    probs = torch.softmax(outputs.logits, dim=-1)
    preds = probs.argmax(dim=-1)

# For training, HuggingFace Trainer handles the loop:
# from transformers import Trainer, TrainingArguments
# training_args = TrainingArguments(
#     output_dir="./results",
#     per_device_train_batch_size=32,
#     num_train_epochs=3,
#     learning_rate=2e-5,
#     fp16=True  # mixed precision for speed
# )
# trainer = Trainer(
#     model=model,
#     args=training_args,
#     train_dataset=sst2_dataset,  # HuggingFace Dataset object
# )
# trainer.train()

# ponytail: This uses the full transformers Trainer API which
# handles gradient accumulation, LR scheduling, and eval loops.
# For production, consider ONNX export or DistilBERT for lower latency.

sections.title: Seq2Seq & Attention Mechanisms
sections.description:
Encoder-decoder RNN: Encoder reads x₁..xn → context vector c = hn (last hidden state). Decoder generates y₁..ym conditioned on c. The information bottleneck at c limits performance for long sequences — a single vector must summarize the entire source.

Bahdanau attention (2015): Context vector ct = Σ αt,i · hi where αt,i = exp(et,i) / Σ exp(et,k) and et,i = v · tanh(Wa · st−1 + Ua · hi). The alignment score et,i measures how well the source word at position i matches the decoder state at time t. Attention is dynamic — each decoder step receives a different weighted combination of encoder states. This is additive attention (the score uses a feed-forward network with tanh).

Luong attention (2015): Simpler formulation — ct = Σ αt,i · hi where αt,i = softmax(stT · hi). Three variants: (1) dot: stT · hi, (2) general: stT · Wa · hi, (3) concat: v · tanh(Wa[st; hi]). Luong uses the decoder's current state st (not previous st−1 like Bahdanau). It also proposes local attention — predict an aligned position pt and attend to a window around it, reducing computation from O(n) to O(2D+1) where D is the window radius.

Teacher forcing: Feed ground truth token as decoder input during training (instead of previous prediction). This speeds convergence but creates exposure bias — the model never sees its own errors at training time. Scheduled sampling mixes ground truth and predicted tokens with a decaying probability.

Beam search: Maintain top-k hypotheses at each decoding step. Beam width k=3–10 for NMT. A score for each hypothesis: log P(y₁..yt|x). Length normalization: divide by |y|α (typical α=0.6–1.0) to avoid bias toward short sequences. Common practice: normalize by sequence length or use a penalty term.

[Intuition]: "Without attention, the encoder must compress the entire source sentence into one vector — like summarizing a book into one sentence. Attention lets the decoder peek back at the source at each step, retrieving relevant context dynamically."

[Concrete Numbers]: "Bahdanau attention improved BLEU on English-French WMT from ~20 to ~28. Adding attention to an LSTM encoder-decoder for WMT'14 English-German took BLEU from ~16 to ~25 — a 50% relative improvement."
sections.codeBlock:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

class BahdanauAttention(nn.Module):
    """Additive attention (Bahdanau et al. 2015).
    alignment: e_{t,i} = v_a · tanh(W_a · s_{t-1} + U_a · h_i)
    """
    def __init__(self, hidden_dim):
        super().__init__()
        self.W_a = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, hidden_dim, bias=False)
        self.U_a = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, hidden_dim, bias=False)
        self.v_a = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, 1, bias=False)

    def forward(self, decoder_state, encoder_outputs, mask=None):
        """
        decoder_state:  (B, H)  — s_{t-1} (previous decoder state)
        encoder_outputs: (B, S, H) — all h_i
        mask:           (B, S) padding mask
        Returns: context (B, H), attention_weights (B, S)
        """
        # Expand decoder state for every source position
        # score: (B, S, H) = tanh(W_a · s_{t-1} + U_a · h_i)
        dec_expanded = decoder_state.unsqueeze(1).expand(-1,
                         encoder_outputs.size(1), -1)          # (B, S, H)
        score = torch.tanh(self.W_a(dec_expanded) +
                           self.U_a(encoder_outputs))           # (B, S, H)

        # Alignment scores e_{t,i}: (B, S, 1) -> (B, S)
        energy = self.v_a(score).squeeze(-1)                   # (B, S)

        # Mask padded positions (set to -inf before softmax)
        if mask is not None:
            energy = energy.masked_fill(mask == 0, -1e9)

        # Attention weights alpha_{t,i}
        attn_weights = F.softmax(energy, dim=-1)               # (B, S)

        # Context vector: weighted sum of encoder outputs
        context = torch.bmm(attn_weights.unsqueeze(1),
                            encoder_outputs).squeeze(1)        # (B, H)
        return context, attn_weights

# --- Luong Dot-Product Attention (simpler alternative) ---
class LuongAttention(nn.Module):
    """Dot-product attention (Luong et al. 2015).
    alignment: score(s_t, h_i) = s_t^T · W_a · h_i
    Uses current decoder state s_t (not s_{t-1} like Bahdanau).
    """
    def __init__(self, hidden_dim):
        super().__init__()
        self.W_a = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, hidden_dim, bias=False)

    def forward(self, decoder_state, encoder_outputs, mask=None):
        # energy = s_t^T · W_a · h_i
        energy = torch.bmm(
            decoder_state.unsqueeze(1),                         # (B, 1, H)
            self.W_a(encoder_outputs).transpose(1, 2)           # (B, H, S)
        ).squeeze(1)                                            # (B, S)
        if mask is not None:
            energy = energy.masked_fill(mask == 0, -1e9)
        attn_weights = F.softmax(energy, dim=-1)
        context = torch.bmm(attn_weights.unsqueeze(1),
                            encoder_outputs).squeeze(1)
        return context, attn_weights

sections.title: Transformer Pre-training — BERT & Its Variants
sections.description:
BERT (Devlin 2019, Google): Bidirectional encoder pre-trained on BooksCorpus (800M words) + English Wikipedia (2.5B words). Two objectives: Masked Language Model (MLM) — 15% of tokens masked: 80% replaced with [MASK], 10% random token, 10% unchanged. The model predicts the original token. Next Sentence Prediction (NSP) — predict if sentence B follows sentence A (binary classification). BERT-base: 12 layers, 768 hidden, 12 heads, 110M params. BERT-large: 24 layers, 1024 hidden, 16 heads, 340M params. Pre-training cost: 4 days on 16 TPUv2 chips (BERT-base).

RoBERTa (Liu 2019, Meta): Robustly Optimized BERT — same architecture, key changes: (1) 160GB of training data (vs 13GB for BERT), (2) dynamic masking (mask differently each epoch), (3) removed NSP (ablation showed it hurts performance), (4) larger batch size (8K vs 256), (5) longer training (500K steps). Outperforms BERT on every GLUE task.

DistilBERT (Sanh 2019, HuggingFace): Knowledge distillation — student model (6 layers, 768 hidden, 66M params) trained on teacher's (BERT-base) softmax probabilities. Uses a triple loss: distillation loss (KL divergence with teacher), masked LM loss, and cosine embedding loss. 40% smaller, 60% faster, retaining 97% of BERT's performance on GLUE.

ALBERT (Lan 2020, Google): A Lite BERT — (1) factorized embedding: V×H → V×E + E×H (E << H), reducing embedding params from V×H to V×E + E×H. (2) cross-layer parameter sharing — all 12 layers share the same weights. (3) Sentence Order Prediction (SOP) instead of NSP — harder task that requires discourse understanding. ALBERT-xxlarge has 235M params (vs BERT-large 340M) with better performance.

ELECTRA (Clark 2020, Stanford): Replaced Token Detection — two models: a small generator (MLM) replaces some tokens, and a discriminator predicts which positions were replaced. The discriminator learns from all tokens (not just masked ones), making training more efficient. ELECTRA-400K matches RoBERTa-1.5M performance at 1/4 the computation.

DeBERTa (He 2020, Microsoft): Decoding-enhanced BERT with disentangled attention — separate vectors for content and relative position. The attention score is: (content-to-content) + (content-to-position) + (position-to-content). Enhanced mask decoder (EMD) replaces [MASK] with absolute position embeddings during fine-tuning. DeBERTa-base outperforms RoBERTa-base on GLUE (88.0 vs 87.6).

[Intuition]: "RoBERTa showed that BERT's NSP task was nearly useless — dynamic masking and more data gave the real gains. Always question whether your pre-training objective actually forces the model to learn the desired capability."

[Concrete Numbers]: "RoBERTa-large achieves 88.5% on GLUE (vs BERT-large 86.5%). Training cost: ~$5K in compute (64 V100 GPUs for 1 day). DistilBERT at 66M params runs 2x faster than BERT-base (110M) with just 3% GLUE degradation — a practical choice for production latency budgets."
sections.codeBlock:
from transformers import (
    AutoTokenizer,
    AutoModelForMaskedLM,
    TrainingArguments,
    Trainer
)

# RoBERTa fine-tuning for a classification task
# Similar API as BERT, but uses different tokenizer (BPE via GPT-2)
model_name = "roberta-base"  # 12 layers, 768 hidden, 125M params
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
model = AutoModelForMaskedLM.from_pretrained(model_name)

# Pre-training data collator for MLM
from transformers import DataCollatorForLanguageModeling

data_collator = DataCollatorForLanguageModeling(
    tokenizer=tokenizer,
    mlm=True,
    mlm_probability=0.15  # mask 15% of tokens
)

# Training setup
training_args = TrainingArguments(
    output_dir="./roberta-pretrain",
    per_device_train_batch_size=32,
    per_device_eval_batch_size=32,
    learning_rate=1e-4,
    weight_decay=0.01,
    num_train_epochs=3,
    fp16=True,
    logging_steps=100,
    save_steps=5000,
    gradient_accumulation_steps=2,  # effective batch = 32*2 = 64
    warmup_steps=10000
)

trainer = Trainer(
    model=model,
    args=training_args,
    train_dataset=pretrain_dataset,  # HuggingFace Dataset of token IDs
    data_collator=data_collator
)

# trainer.train()
#
# For GLUE fine-tuning after pre-training:
# AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained("roberta-base")

sections.title: LLM Fine-Tuning — Parameter-Efficient Methods
sections.description:
Full fine-tuning updates all model parameters. For GPT-3 175B: each update requires 175B params × 4 bytes × 2 (forward + backward) = 1.4TB for activations, plus 350GB of gradients, plus 1.4TB for Adam optimizer states (2× momentum + variance). This requires hundreds of GPUs — impractical for most teams.

LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) (Hu 2021): W' = W + BA where B ∈ ℝd×r, A ∈ ℝr×k, r << min(d,k). Only A and B are trainable — W is frozen. For attention projection matrices (WQ, WK, WV, WO), apply LoRA with r=4–16. Training memory drops from ~56GB to ~10GB for a 7B model (QLoRA 4-bit). At inference, merge W←W+BA (no overhead). Scaling factor: α/r (α is a constant, typically 16–32, not a learning rate — just scales the LoRA update magnitude).

QLoRA (Dettmers 2023): LoRA + 4-bit NormalFloat (NF4) quantization — optimal quantization for normally distributed weights. Double quantization: quantize the quantization constants themselves. Paged optimizers: use CPU memory for optimizer states during gradient checkpointing to avoid OOM. Fine-tune 65B model on a single 48GB GPU.

Prefix tuning: Prepend learnable continuous vectors ("virtual tokens") to the input. The prefix (typically 10–200 tokens) is trained while the rest of the model stays frozen. P-tuning v2: repurpose prefix tuning for all model layers instead of just input — matches full fine-tuning performance on sequence labeling tasks.

Adapter: Bottleneck layers inserted between Transformer sub-layers: down-project (d→h), non-linearity (ReLU/GELU), up-project (h→d). The hidden dimension h << d (typically h=64–256). Each adapter adds 3–5% of total parameters. Adapters can be stacked or swapped per task (modular). Higher inference latency than LoRA because adapter computations cannot be merged into the base weights.

[Intuition]: "LoRA works because fine-tuning weight updates have low intrinsic rank — you don't need the full parameter space (d×k) to shift the model to a new task. A rank-16 matrix (d×16 + 16×k) captures the direction of the update with ~99% of the effectiveness."

[Concrete Numbers]: "Fine-tuning LLaMA 2 7B: full fine-tune needs 56GB GPU memory (16GB weights + 16GB gradients + 24GB Adam states). QLoRA 4-bit with rank 16 needs only ~10GB memory with <0.5% per-quality loss on most benchmarks (MMLU, HellaSwag, GSM8K). Training time: 3 hours vs 10 hours full fine-tune on a single A100 (80GB) for a 7B model."
sections.codeBlock:
import torch
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer
from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model, TaskType

# Load base model (4-bit quantized for memory efficiency)
model_name = "meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-hf"
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    model_name,
    load_in_4bit=True,  # NF4 quantization saves ~75% memory
    torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    device_map="auto"     # distribute across available GPUs
)
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)

# LoRA configuration
lora_config = LoraConfig(
    r=8,                     # rank — typically 4-64; higher = more capacity
    lora_alpha=32,           # scaling factor: alpha/r (here 32/8 = 4)
    target_modules=["q_proj", "k_proj", "v_proj", "o_proj"],
    lora_dropout=0.1,
    bias="none",             # don't train bias terms
    task_type=TaskType.CAUSAL_LM
)

# Apply LoRA (freezes base model, adds trainable adapters)
model = get_peft_model(model, lora_config)

# Print trainable params (should be ~0.1-1% of total)
model.print_trainable_parameters()
# Output example: "trainable params: 4.2M || all params: 6.7B || trainable: 0.06%"

# Training loop (HuggingFace Trainer or custom)
# Only the LoRA adapters receive gradients
# optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=2e-4)
# ... train as usual ...

# Save only the LoRA weights (tiny: ~8MB for r=8 on 7B)
# model.save_pretrained("./lora-adapters")

# Inference: optionally merge LoRA into base weights
# from peft import PeftModel
# model = PeftModel.from_pretrained(base_model, "./lora-adapters")
# merged = model.merge_and_unload()  # W += BA, no overhead at inference

sections.title: FAANG Interview Q&A — NLP Deep Dive
sections.description:
[Interview Angle] NLP Interview Questions with Detailed Answers

Q1 (Google L4): "Explain the difference between CBOW and Skip-gram in Word2Vec — which one is better for rare words?"

A: CBOW predicts the center word from its surrounding context (bag of words). Skip-gram predicts the surrounding context words from the center word. The key difference: Skip-gram generates one training example per (center, context) pair, while CBOW combines all context words into one example. For a sentence of length N with window size k, Skip-gram produces O(N·k) training examples; CBOW produces O(N) examples. Skip-gram works better for rare words because each occurrence of a rare word as center generates multiple context-word prediction tasks, giving the model more chances to learn its representation. CBOW is faster and works well for frequent words. On the analogy task, Skip-gram achieves ~75% accuracy vs CBOW's ~65%.

Q2 (Meta E5): "Why is a CRF layer beneficial for NER on top of a BiLSTM or BERT?"

A: NER labels follow strong transition constraints: I-PER must follow B-PER or I-PER (BIO scheme). Without CRF, a per-token softmax can predict an illegal transition (e.g., B-PER followed by I-ORG). The CRF models the joint probability of the entire label sequence, incorporating a transition matrix P(yi|yi−1) learned from data. During decoding (Viterbi), it finds the globally optimal sequence — not just the argmax per position. The CRF loss also improves training by considering the full partition function. In practice, BiLSTM-CRF adds ~2% F1 over BiLSTM-softmax on CoNLL-2003. For BERT-based models, the gain is smaller (~0.5% F1) because BERT already captures strong contextual features, but CRF still eliminates impossible label transitions at inference.

Q3 (Google L5): "Explain the attention mechanism in Seq2Seq — how does Bahdanau attention differ from Luong attention?"

A: Both compute a context vector as a weighted sum of encoder hidden states, but they differ in: (1) When attention is computed: Bahdanau uses the previous decoder state st−1 to compute attention for step t. Luong uses the current decoder state st (requiring an initial prediction without attention first). (2) Alignment function: Bahdanau uses additive attention with a tanh layer: v · tanh(W·st−1 + U·hi). Luong offers three variants: dot (stT · hi), general (stT · W · hi), and concat (similar to Bahdanau). (3) Computational cost: Bahdanau is more expensive due to the feed-forward network. (4) Context fusion: Bahdanau concatenates the context with the decoder input; Luong uses it directly in the output layer. Both achieve similar BLEU scores — the choice is often architectural preference.

Q4 (OpenAI L5): "Compare BERT and GPT — when would you use each?"

A: BERT uses a bidirectional encoder with masked language modeling — it sees the full context (left + right) for each token. This makes it superior for understanding tasks: classification, NER, QA, sentiment analysis. GPT uses a unidirectional (causal) decoder with autoregressive language modeling — it predicts the next token given only left-side context. This makes it ideal for generation tasks: text completion, summarization, dialogue, code generation. For tasks that need both understanding and generation (e.g., abstractive QA, summarization), encoder-decoder models like T5 or BART are often preferred. Currently (2024+), large GPT-style models (GPT-4, Claude, Llama) at 100B+ scale demonstrate emergent abilities (reasoning, in-context learning) that BERT-style encoders don't show, making them the default for most general-purpose NLP tasks. Use BERT for: fast, efficient, bidirectional representations on structured tasks. Use GPT for: open-ended generation, few-shot learning, and tasks requiring reasoning.

Q5 (Meta L5 / OpenAI L5): "How does LoRA work and why is it effective?"

A: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) hypothesizes that the weight update ΔW during fine-tuning has low intrinsic rank. Instead of updating the full weight matrix W ∈ ℝd×k, LoRA decomposes the update into two low-rank matrices: ΔW = BA where B ∈ ℝd×r and A ∈ ℝr×k with r << min(d,k). W is frozen; only B and A are trained. For a 7B model with d=4096, k=4096, r=16: full fine-tune updates 16.8M params (4096²) per matrix, LoRA updates only 131K (2×4096×16) — a 128× reduction. Applied to Q, K, V, O projection matrices, LoRA typically trains <0.2% of total parameters. Why it's effective: (1) The intrinsic dimension of fine-tuning tasks is low — you don't need the full rank. (2) B and A can be merged into W after training (zero inference overhead). (3) Multiple task-specific LoRA adapters can be hot-swapped without duplicating the base model. (4) Combined with quantization (QLoRA), you can fine-tune 65B models on a single GPU. Ablation: rank 1 often works, rank 8 is typical, rank 64 rarely helps more than rank 16.

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Mikolov, T., et al. "Efficient Estimation of Word Representations in Vector Space." (Word2Vec) — 2013
  • Mikolov, T., et al. "Distributed Representations of Words and Phrases and their Compositionality." (Negative Sampling) — 2013
  • Pennington, J., et al. "GloVe: Global Vectors for Word Representation." — 2014
  • Bojanowski, P., et al. "Enriching Word Vectors with Subword Information." (FastText) — 2016
  • Kim, Y. "Convolutional Neural Networks for Sentence Classification." — 2014
  • Bahdanau, D., et al. "Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and Translate." (Attention) — 2015
  • Luong, M., et al. "Effective Approaches to Attention-based Neural Machine Translation." — 2015
  • Vaswani, A., et al. "Attention Is All You Need." (Transformer) — 2017
  • Devlin, J., et al. "BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers." — 2019
  • Liu, Y., et al. "RoBERTa: A Robustly Optimized BERT Pretraining Approach." — 2019
  • Sanh, V., et al. "DistilBERT, a Distilled Version of BERT." — 2019
  • Yang, Z., et al. "Hierarchical Attention Networks for Document Classification." — 2016
  • Hu, E., et al. "LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models." — 2021
  • Dettmers, T., et al. "QLoRA: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized Language Models." — 2023
  • Lample, G., et al. "Neural Architectures for Named Entity Recognition." (BiLSTM-CRF) — 2016
